coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots derived from lignin nanoparticles for dual UV-excited fluorescent anti-counterfeiting materials
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Tianning
He, Yingying
Wang, Chunyu
Bi, Hongfu
Chen, Gang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots are widely used as fluorescent materials due to their excellent photostability and low toxicity. However, the majority of carbon quantum dots were limited by a single anti-counterfeiting effect excited by ultraviolet A (UVA, 320–400 nm) light. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from lignin were developed through the doping of nitrogen, phosphorus, or boron using a one-pot hydrothermal method. These CQDs could show yellow (Y-CQDs) and blue-violet (B-CQDs) fluorescent colors under UVA and ultraviolet C (UVC, 100–280 nm) stimulations, respectively. Via mixing these two types of carbon quantum dots, the mixed dispersion could be used as a printing paste or prepared a film with polyvinyl alcohol. Benefiting from the synergistic effect of two carbon quantum dots, the resultant products could emit blue-purple and yellow-green fluorescence colors under 254 nm and 365 nm UV irradiations, respectively, thereby exhibiting dual anti-counterfeiting capabilities. This work paves a new path for developing lignin-based carbon quantum dots that show a promising application for anti-counterfeiting capabilities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin
Carbon quantum dots
Dual anti-counterfeiting material

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of lignin-derived carbon quantum dots
Preparation of dual anti-counterfeiting paste and film
Morphology and chemical structure of B-CQDs and Y-CQDs
Optical properties of B-CQDs and Y-CQDs
The synergistic effect of B-CQDs and Y-CQDs for dual anti-counterfeiting application

State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: A volunteer assisted in completing the real-time motion monitoring experiment, and informed consent was obtained for publishing the images and data. The authors acknowledge the financial support of the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFC1902102), State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering at South China University of Technology (2020ZD02), and the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Project General Program (2021A1515010538).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Digital photo and (b) the Zeta potential of the aqueous dispersion of lignin nanoparticles. (c) The particle size distribution of lignin nanoparticles. (d) SEM image, (e) TEM image and (f) HR-TEM image of lignin nanoparticles.
(a) TEM image, (b) HR-TEM image, and (c) the particle size distribution of B-CQDs. (d) TEM image, (e) HR-TEM image, and (f) the particle size distribution of Y-CQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra, (b) Raman spectra, and (c) XRD patterns of B-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and lignin nanoparticles.

(a) XPS total spectrum of B-CQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) P2p, (d) O1s, and (e) B1s of B-CQDs. (f) XPS total spectrum of Y-CQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (g) C1s, (h) P2p, and (i) O1s of Y-CQDs.

(a) UV–visible absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra, (b) emission spectra under different wavelengths, and (c) three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of B-CQDs. (d) UV–visible absorption and PL spectra, (e) emission spectra under different wavelengths, and (f) three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of Y-CQDs.

(a) Digital photos and (b) fluorescence spectra of B-CQDs under different pH values. (c) Digital photos and (d) fluorescence spectra of Y-CQDs under different pH values. (e–f) Fluorescence intensity loss plots of B-CQDs and Y-CQDs after storing (e) for 90 days at room temperature and (f) for 40 h at UV irradiation.
Preparation of dual anti-counterfeiting materials based on lignin-based B-CQDs and Y-CQDs.
Synthesis routes of two lignin-based carbon quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots derived from lignin nanoparticles for dual UV-excited fluorescent anti-counterfeiting materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Engineering Technology Research and Development Center of Specialty Paper and Paper-Based Functional Materials, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Engineering Technology Research and Development Center of Specialty Paper and Paper-Based Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Engineering Technology Research and Development Center of Specialty Paper and Paper-Based Functional Materials, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering, South China University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots are widely used as fluorescent materials due to their excellent photostability and low toxicity. However, the majority of carbon quantum dots were limited by a single anti-counterfeiting effect excited by ultraviolet A (UVA, 320–400 nm) light. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from lignin were developed through the doping of nitrogen, phosphorus, or boron using a one-pot hydrothermal method. These CQDs could show yellow (Y-CQDs) and blue-violet (B-CQDs) fluorescent colors under UVA and ultraviolet C (UVC, 100–280 nm) stimulations, respectively. Via mixing these two types of carbon quantum dots, the mixed dispersion could be used as a printing paste or prepared a film with polyvinyl alcohol. Benefiting from the synergistic effect of two carbon quantum dots, the resultant products could emit blue-purple and yellow-green fluorescence colors under 254 nm and 365 nm UV irradiations, respectively, thereby exhibiting dual anti-counterfeiting capabilities. This work paves a new path for developing lignin-based carbon quantum dots that show a promising application for anti-counterfeiting capabilities.

ce:section.ce:para: Wood fiber resources, as a rich and renewable biomass resource, have great potential as the next generation of energy and material sources. Among the components of wood fiber, lignin ranks second only to cellulose in content, with over 70 million tons produced annually through biomass separation processes [1]. The lignin is an aromatic natural polymer that consists of three basic structural units, including guaiacyl (G type), syringyl (S type), and hydroxyphenyl (H type) [2]. It possesses various functional groups, such as carboxyl, double bonds, methoxy, benzene rings, phenolic hydroxyl, and alcoholic hydroxyl groups, making it prone to various chemical modifications (e.g. oxidation, reduction, hydroxymethylation, acetylation, amination, sulfonation, phenolation, epoxidation, alkylation, and graft copolymerization, etc.) [3–5]. Compared with lignin, the dispersibility of lignin nanoparticles in water is greatly improved, which overcomes the shortcomings of lignin insoluble in water to a certain extent [6]. At the same time, more functional groups are exposed on the surface of lignin nanoparticles [7]. These advantages enable the lignin nanoparticles to be used as an ideal precursor for preparing carbon quantum dots.

Compared to traditional fluorescent materials, carbon quantum dots as fluorescent nanomaterials possess a unique graphene-like lattice structure and exhibit strong photostability, chemical inertness, low toxicity, and biocompatibility [8]. These carbon quantum dots can be widely used in the fields of chemical sensing [9], biosensing [10], drug delivery [11], photocatalysis [12], bioimaging [13], and nanomedicine [14]. For example, He et al. used celery as carbon source to prepare carbon quantum dots. The carbon quantum dots could produce reactive oxygen in a weak acid environment and eliminate reactive oxygen near physiological pH, achieving pH-selective conversion for antibacterial/wound healing [15]. Li et al. constructed a bionic solar-powered seawater evaporator based on the lignin-based carbon quantum dots modified chitosan carbon aerogel. The introduction of lignin-based carbon quantum dots improved the hydrophilic properties and light absorption capacity of chitosan aerogel biochar, which made the evaporator exhibit excellent evaporation rate and photothermal conversion efficiency [16]. Moreover, doping new atoms into carbon quantum dots can create more active sites and alter their electronic structure, thereby endowing them with multiple fluorescence properties [17–19]. For instance, the atomic radius of nitrogen is close to that of carbon, and the nitrogen atom has five valence electrons that can form strong bonds with carbon atoms, thus which is commonly used as a n-type doping atom in the preparation of carbon quantum dots [20]. By contrast, the phosphorus atom has a large atomic radius and is easy to cause structural defects during the doping process, leading to the alteration of carbon quantum dots' electronic and optical properties [21]. Compared to other heteroatoms, the boron atom as a p-type dopant shows lower electronegativity than the carbon atom [22,23]. Therefore, doping carbon quantum dots with N, P, and B atoms can tune their electronic structure, generate more surface defects as active sites, and obtain new energy levels, which can achieve novel photoluminescence properties.

Recently, lignin-based carbon quantum dots were prepared through the acidification of lignin using o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid via hydrothermal method [24]. These lignin-based carbon quantum dots can emit blue fluorescence color under 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation, suitable for writing or blending with fibers as the anti-counterfeiting materials, but they were limited by a single anti-counterfeiting effect. Then, the lignin-based carbon quantum dots with dual anti-counterfeiting capabilities were developed through a two-step acidification and hydrothermal treatment. Although these carbon quantum dots could show yellowish-green fluorescence color under 365 nm and 395 nm excitations, the color difference was minimal and difficult to distinguish [25]. In addition, triple-emission carbon quantum dots were prepared by acidified lignin with p-aminophenylboronic acid and were used to print on the paper, but the printed pattern could be seen with the eyes under natural light. What's more, these carbon quantum dots could emit at 346 nm, 428 nm, and 514 nm under 300 nm, 330 nm, and 490 nm of excitations, respectively. However, both the 490 nm of excitation and the 514 nm of emission lights are visible light, and their similar colors are prone to causing interference [26].

In this work, the lignin-based carbon quantum dots (e.g., B-CQDs and Y-CQDs) were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method, where lignin nanoparticles were used as the precursor, and m-phenylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine, phosphoric acid, and sodium cyanoborohydride were acted as nitrogen, phosphorus, and boron dopants, respectively. B-CQDs can emit purple and blue fluorescence colors under 254 nm and 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation, respectively, and Y-CQDs can emit weak yellow and strong yellow fluorescence colors under 254 nm and 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation, respectively. As B-CQDs and Y-CQDs were mixed, the resulting carbon quantum dots emitted blue-violet and yellow-green fluorescence colors under 254 nm and 365 nm excitations, respectively (Scheme 1 ). This mixed dispersion can be printed on papers or integrated with polyvinyl alcohol to prepare films, achieving dual-emission anti-counterfeiting property and addressing the limitations of traditional single-emission fluorescent anti-counterfeiting.

Kraft lignin was supplied by UPM Co., Ltd. (Finland). Sodium cyanoborohydride, m-phenylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) 1799, and phosphoric acid were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China). Dialysis bags (300 Da and 7000 Da) were obtained from Viskase Co., Ltd. (USA), and the filters with 0.22 μm aperture were procured from Tianjin Navigation Experimental Equipment Co., Ltd. (China).

Lignin nanoparticles were prepared using the anti-solvent method combined with ultrasonic treatment. 0.5 g of kraft lignin was dissolved in an ethanol and glycerol mixed solvent of 100 g, where the mass ratio of ethanol to glycerol was 1:3. Then, the mixture was filtered to remove insoluble impurities and was dialyzed using a 7000 Da dialysis bag for 72 h. The dispersion in the dialysis bag was ultrasonicated using a cell disruptor with 200 W power for 5 min under an ice bath and further was freeze-dried to obtain lignin nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of lignin-derived carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare B-CQDs, 0.1 g of lignin nanoparticles, 0.1 g of p-phenylenediamine, 0.06 g of sodium cyanoborohydride, 2.0 g of phosphoric acid, and 18.0 ml of deionized water were added into a 50 ml polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal reactor. Then, the above mixture was ultrasonically dispersed for 30 min and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the resultant product was cooled down to room temperature naturally and was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the supernatant was dialyzed for 72 h and was freeze-dried to obtain B-CQDs powder. Similarly, Y-CQDs were prepared through that 0.1 g of lignin nanoparticles, 0.1 g of m-phenylenediamine, 5.0 g of phosphoric acid, and 15.0 ml of deionized water were added into the polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal reactor. This mixture was treated with the similar processes as for B-CQDs. The effects of hydrothermal reaction time, reaction temperature, and the phosphoric acid concentration on the B-CQDs and Y-CQDs were investigated to obtain optimal lignin-based carbon quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of dual anti-counterfeiting paste and film
ce:section.ce:para: The B-CQDs and Y-CQD were mixed with 1.5 wt% sodium carboxymethyl cellulose dispersion, where the mass ratio of B-CQDs to Y-CQD was 3:2. Then, the mixture was dispersed under stirring with high-speed for 2 h to obtain screen printing anti-counterfeiting paste. Finally, this paste was printed on non-fluorescent paper to obtain anti-counterfeiting patterns. Similarly, the B-CQDs together with Y-CQDs (the B-CQDs/Y-CQD mass ratio of 3:2) were added into a 2 wt% PVA aqueous solution, and this mixture was dispersed evenly under stirring intensely for 2 h. Subsequently, the mixture was injected into a mold and dried to obtain a transparent anti-counterfeiting film.

The morphologies of lignin nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots were examined using a field emission scanning microscope (SEM, SU5000, Hitachi, Japan) and a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, Jem-2100F, JEOL, Japan). The functional groups of the lignin nanoparticles and the carbon quantum dots were characterized using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Tensor 27, Bruker, Germany). The chemical composition of the carbon quantum dots was assessed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha, ThermoFisher, USA). The crystal structures of the lignin nanoparticles and the carbon quantum dots were analyzed using a Raman spectrometer (LabRAM Odyssey, Horiba, Japan) and an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, X'Pert Powder, PANalytical, Netherlands). The ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of the carbon quantum dots were measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-1900, Shimadzu, Japan). The Zeta potential of B-CQDs and Y-CQDs and the average particle size of lignin nanoparticles were measured by Malvern Zetasizer Nano-NS90 (ZSU3100, UK) at 25 °C. Photoluminescence (PL) properties of the carbon quantum dots were evaluated using a fluorescence spectrometer (FluoroMax-4, Horiba, Japan). The 3D fluorescence spectra were obtained using a synchronous absorption-3D fluorescence spectrometer (Aqualog, Horiba, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime (TR-PL) and absolute quantum yield (QY) were measured with a steady-state-transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh, UK).

A homogeneous aqueous dispersion of lignin nanoparticles was shown in Fig. 1a, with a Zeta potential of −31.8 mV (Fig. 1b). This result indicated that the lignin nanoparticles exhibited good dispersibility in water. Their average particle size was measured by the Malvern Zetasizer Nano-NS90 at 25 °C, showing about 49.7 nm (Fig. 1c). The morphologies of lignin nanoparticles were revealed by a field emission scanning microscopy (SEM) and a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) as shown in Fig. 1d–f. The lignin nanoparticles were an approximately spherical.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical structure of B-CQDs and Y-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Two lignin-based carbon quantum dots, including B-CQDs and Y-CQDs, were synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method. The aqueous dispersions of both carbon quantum dots were yellow (Fig. 2a and d insets). The B-CQDs and the Y-CQDs showed −21.34 mV and − 23.06 mV of the Zeta potential, respectively (Fig. S1a–b), indicating their excellent dispersity in water. Their morphologies were revealed by HR-TEM, and the average particle sizes were calculated using Image J software (Fig. 2a–f). The average particle sizes of B-CQDs and Y-CQDs were about 3.7 and 4.3 nm, respectively. As shown in the HR-TEM images (Fig. 1b and e), both B-CQDs and Y-CQDs demonstrate well-resolved lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which correspond to the graphene (100) plane spacing [27,28].

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy analysis was conducted on B-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and lignin nanoparticles, as illustrated in Fig. 3a. The broad peaks in the range of 3250–3750 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds [21,29]. The stretching vibrations of CH bonds was observed at 2932 cm−1 [30]. The peak at 526 cm−1 was attributed to the PC stretching vibration, and the peak at 1162 cm−1 was ascribed to the PO stretching vibration [31]. The presence of a peak at 1289 cm−1 indicated the existence of CN bonds [32]. The peaks in the range of 990–1000 cm−1 were belonged to the stretching vibrations of P–O–C bonds [33]. In particular, the peak at 929 cm−1 in the B-CQDs was attributed to the stretching vibration of B–O–H [34], confirming successful boron doping. As for Y-CQDs, the peak at 959 cm−1 was corresponded to a benzene ring [35], and the peak at 906 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibraation of P–O–R, where the R represents a benzene ring [36]. This result indicates that the Y-CQDs incorporate a benzene ring structure successfully.

Moreover, the Raman spectra of B-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and lignin nanoparticles displayed two prominent D and G peaks at 1352 cm−1 and 1576 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 3b). This suggests that the B-CQDs and the Y-CQDs contain both sp2-hybridized and sp3-hybridized carbon, corresponding to the characteristic peaks of graphene and its disordered vibration peaks, respectively [28,37].

Additionally, the crystalline structures of B-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and lignin nanoparticles were investigated using X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 3c, the 2θ value of lignin nanoparticles was 18.4°, with an interlayer spacing of 4.81 Å. Compared to lignin nanoparticles, the 2θ values of the B-CQDs and the Y-CQDs shifted to 23.0° and 23.2°, and their interlayer spacings were 3.86 Å and 3.83 Å, respectively, indicating a more ordered structure. The peaks for both types of CQDs were similar to the (002) peak of graphene [24,38]. The results from the Raman and XRD analyses indicate that the lignin nanoparticles were converted into carbon quantum dots with ordered graphite structure through hydrothermal reaction.

To further investigate the elemental composition of the B-CQDs and the Y-CQDs, the valence states of the elements in B-CQDs and Y-CQDs were analyzed by the XPS spectrum (Figs. 4a–i and S2a–b). In the XPS full spectrum of B-CQDs (Fig. 4a), binding energies of 297.95 eV, 143.95 eV, 544.95 eV, 409.95 eV, and 195.95 eV were corresponded to C1s, P2p, O1s, N1s, and B1s, with relative contents of 35.84 %, 13.95 %, 32.60 %, 3.18 %, and 14.43 %, respectively. Specifically, the high-resolution C1s XPS spectra exhibited three peaks at 284.65 eV, 285.40 eV, and 286.65 eV, corresponding to C–C/C=C, C–O/C–N/C–P, and CO, respectively (Fig. 4b) [31,39,40]. Two peaks at 133.70 eV and 134.65 eV belonged to the PO and PC peaks in the high-resolution P2p XPS spectra, respectively (Fig. 4c) [21]. The C=O/P=O, BO, and C–O/P–O peaks occurred at 531.40 eV, 532.55 eV, and 532.90 eV in the high-resolution O1s XPS spectra (Fig. 4d) [21,39]. The high-resolution N1s XPS spectra exhibited the CN and NH peaks at 399.55 eV and 401.05 eV (Fig. S2a), respectively [25,41]. The peaks at 190.80 eV and 191.60 eV were corresponded to BC and BO peaks in the high-resolution B1s XPS spectra (Fig. 4e) [31]. As shown in the XPS full spectrum of the Y-CQDs (Fig. 4f), the C1s, P2p, O1s, and N1s could be observed at the binding energies of 291.29 eV, 143.95 eV, 544.95 eV, and 409.95 eV, with relative contents of 32.30 %, 15.94 %, 51.03 %, and 0.73 %, respectively. Similarly, the high-resolution C1s XPS spectra exhibited the C–C/C=C, C–O/C–N/C–P, and CO peaks at 284.65 eV, 285.00 eV, and 286.40 eV, respectively (Fig. 4g). Two peaks at 133.10 eV and 133.95 eV were corresponded to PO and PC peaks in the high-resolution P2p XPS spectra (Fig. 4h). The high-resolution O1s XSP spectra showed C=O/P=O and C–O/P–O peaks at 530.95 eV and 532.55 eV, respectively (Fig. 4i). The peaks at 399.85 eV and 401.95 eV were attributed to CN and NH in the high-resolution N1s XPS spectra (Fig. S2b). The XPS analysis indicated that B-CQDs and Y-CQDs were doped by the phosphorus (P) and nitrogen (N) elements, specially, and B-CQDs were also doped by the boron (B) element. The XPS analysis is consistent with the results obtained by FTIR, Raman, and XRD.

To obtain optimal B-CQDs and Y-CQDs, the effects of the size of lignin particle, hydrothermal time, hydrothermal temperature, and phosphoric acid content on their fluorescence intensity were investigated, respectively (Figs. S3a–b and S4a–f). As shown in Fig. S3a–b, the carbon quantum dots prepared from lignin nanoparticles (labeled as B-CQDS LNP and Y-CQDs LNP, respectively) showed higher fluorescence intensity than those prepared from kraft lignin (labeled as B-CQDS KL and Y-CQDs KL, respectively). As the hydrothermal time increased from 2 h to 12 h and the hydrothermal temperature increased from 120 °C to 200 °C, the fluorescence intensity of both B-CQDs and Y-CQDs increased. With increasing the content of phosphoric acid from 5 wt% to 10 wt%, the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs was significantly improved, however, it was reduced when further increased the content of phosphoric acid from 10 wt% to 30 wt% (Fig. S4c). The fluorescence intensity of Y-CQDs was increased with the increase of the phosphoric acid's content from 5 wt% to 25 wt%, but it was decreased as increased the phosphoric acid's content to 30 wt%. Thus, the optimal phosphoric acid's contents of B-CQDs and YCQDs were 10 wt% and 25 wt%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of B-CQDs and Y-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of B-CQDs and Y-CQDs were characterized by UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra (Fig. 5a–f). As shown in Fig. 5a, the B-CQDs exhibited a strong absorption peak below 300 nm wavelength due to the π-π transition of the aromatic domain, and the emission wavelength of B-CQDs was 375 nm under 279 nm UV excitation. Under varying excitation wavelengths (Fig. 5b), the emission wavelength of B-CQDs showed a red shift with increasing excitation wavelength, likely because the introduction of phosphorus (P), nitrogen (N), and boron (B) elements led to various surface defects [42,43]. The three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum of B-CQDs was shown in Fig. 5c. The B-CQDs demonstrated a broad emission wavelength range, which is consistent with the findings from the emission spectrum under different excitation wavelengths. By contrast, the Y-CQDs also had a strong absorption peak below 300 nm wavelength, and it showed an absorption peak at 385 nm owing to the n-π transition of CO or CN (Fig. 4d) [25,44]. The emission wavelength of Y-CQDs was 522 nm under 376 nm ultraviolet excitation (Fig. 5d). In particular, the emission wavelength of Y-CQDs had almost no change under varying excitation wavelengths, suggesting that the fluorescence emission arises from fewer surface defects (Fig. 5e) [25,45,46]. Thus, the emission wavelength range of Y-CQDs is narrower than that of B-CQDs (Fig. 5f).

Additionally, time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) tests were conducted on both B-CQDs and Y-CQDs (Fig. S5a–b). The average fluorescence lifetimes of the B-CQDs and the Y-CQDs were 2.73 ns and 3.65 ns, respectively. The absolute quantum yields of the B-CQDs and the Y-CQDs were determined using an integrating sphere (Fig. S6a–b). The B-CQDs showed a higher quantum yield of 10.55 % than that of the Y-CQDs (4.34 %). These results indicate that the incorporation of boron elements in the B-CQDs significantly enhances the fluorescence properties of lignin-based carbon quantum dots.

The effects of environmental factors on the fluorescence stability of the B-CQDs and the Y-CQDs were evaluated (Fig. 6a–f). The color of the B-CQDs dispersion was blue-green under strongly acidic conditions, and it became yellow as the pH value increased gradually. It emitted purple and blue fluorescence colors under 254 nm and 365 nm excitations, respectively (Fig. 6a). The fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs gradually decreased with increasing the pH value from 1.9 to 7.7 (Fig. 6b). This result indicated that the fluorescence property of B-CQDs was gradually quenched in alkaline conditions. The Y-CQDs dispersion showed a yellow color under acidic and alkaline conditions, and it emitted a yellow fluorescence color under both 254 nm and 365 nm excitations (Fig. 6d). By contrast, the Y-CQDs demonstrated superior fluorescence stability at a broad pH value range of 1.2–10.3 (Fig. 6e). But further increasing the pH value greatly reduced the fluorescence intensity of Y-CQDs.

Moreover, the stability of carbon quantum dots is essential for their practical application. The effect of storage time on the stability of B-CQDs and Y-CQDs was investigated. Even after 90 days of storage at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs remained at 90.6 %, and that of Y-CQDs was 94.3 % (Fig. 6c). Furthermore, following 40 h of UV irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs decreased to 90.1 %, and the Y-CQDs maintained a fluorescence intensity of 92.2 % (Fig. 6f). These findings indicate that both B-CQDs and Y-CQDs possess excellent stability, making them suitable for anti-counterfeiting applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synergistic effect of B-CQDs and Y-CQDs for dual anti-counterfeiting application
ce:section.ce:para: Benefiting from the distinct fluorescence colors of B-CQDs and Y-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths, the mixing of B-CQDs and Y-CQDs resulted in a dual-wavelength excitation and a dual-emission effect, enabling it suitable for using as a dual fluorescent anti-counterfeiting material (Fig. 7 ). The fluorescence spectra of the mixed carbon quantum dots were shown in Fig. S7. The maximum emission peak of the mixed carbon quantum dots was 522 nm under 365 nm UV excitation, and a weak peak appeared near 450 nm, which belonged to B-CQDs, so the emission wavelength was blue-shifted under 365 nm UV excitation. The maximum emission peak of the mixed carbon quantum dots was 376 nm under 254 nm UV excitation, and Y-CQDs had almost no effect on the luminescence of the mixed carbon quantum dots under 254 nm UV excitation. For example, a dual anti-counterfeiting film was obtained by adding B-CQDs and Y-CQDs into the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix. This film can exhibit blue-purple and yellow-green fluorescence colors under 254 nm and 365 nm excitations, respectively. In addition, the mixed aqueous dispersion of B-CQDs and Y-CQDs was used as a printing paste and could be printed on non-fluorescent paper using screen printing. In particular, the resulting patterns on the paper were invisible under visible light, while these patterns could emerge under UV irradiation at 254 nm and 365 nm, respectively. Compared with the previously reported lignin-based carbon quantum dots for anti-counterfeiting, such as single UVA band excitation and small fluorescence color difference [25,47,48], the mixed carbon quantum dots in this work demonstrate superior anti-counterfeiting performance.

In summary, the lignin nanoparticles were obtained by the green anti-solvent method, and the nanoscale lignin-based B-CQDs and Y-CQDs were synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method. The Y-CQDs were doped with nitrogen and phosphorus elements, while the B-CQDs were also doped with boron element in addition to doping nitrogen and phosphorus. Compared to the Y-CQDs, the doping of boron element in the B-CQDs not only given the B-CQDs with unique UVC band excitation property, but also enhanced the B-CQDs' quantum yield. The prepared B-CQDs showed an emission wavelength of 375 nm under ultraviolet excitation of 279 nm, and the Y-CQDs exhibited an emission wavelength of 522 nm under ultraviolet excitation of 376 nm. By physically mixing B-CQDs and Y-CQDs, the mixed carbon quantum dots possessed dual fluorescent anti-counterfeiting properties, which emit blue-purple and yellow-green fluorescence colors under 254 nm and 365 nm UV irradiations, respectively. The mixed carbon quantum dots were added to the matrix (e.g. PVA) to prepare fluorescent anti-counterfeiting films or directly used them as printing inks. The films and the printed patterns could show obvious color distinction under dual excitations and have great potential in anti-counterfeiting applications.

Tianning Yang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yingying He: Writing – review & editing. Chunyu Wang: Methodology. Hongfu Bi: Software. Gang Chen: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: A volunteer assisted in completing the real-time motion monitoring experiment, and informed consent was obtained for publishing the images and data. The authors acknowledge the financial support of the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFC1902102), State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering at South China University of Technology (2020ZD02), and the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Project General Program (2021A1515010538).
